title: The Geometry of Clotheslines
text_zh: 从我家阳台看出去，对面居民楼的阳台上挂满了各种各样的衣服。远远看去，它们构成了一幅关于生活的抽象画。白色的床单像巨大的画布，在风中微微鼓起，投下方形的阴影。彩色的衣服像一串串旗帜，赤橙黄绿蓝紫，在灰色的楼体背景下格外鲜明。小孩子的衣服最好认，又小又多，一排排挂着，像音符一样排列得密密麻麻。我能通过晾晒的衣服大致推断出每户人家的家庭结构。三楼左边那户，总是晾着很多男士衬衫和一些小裙子，应该是一个父亲带着女儿住。四楼中间那户，晾的全是深色的厚衣服，大概住着一两个独居的老人。五楼右边那户很有意思，衣服的风格经常变化，有时候运动装，有时候西装，有时候花哨的连衣裙，像是一个衣橱丰富的年轻女性。这些衣服在阳光下慢慢变干的过程，有一种说不出的宁静感。水分一点一点蒸发，织物从深色变浅，从沉重变轻盈，最终在微风中轻轻摆动，像在呼吸。晾衣服是整个城市里最安静的家务劳动，也是最具有展示性的——你把你的生活挂在外面，让所有人看到。但没有人觉得这是暴露隐私。也许因为衣服在晾绳上的样子太平凡了，平凡到成为了一种美。
text_pinyin: Cóng wǒ jiā yángtái kàn chūqù, duìmiàn jūmínlóu de yángtái shàng guà mǎn le gè zhǒng gè yàng de yīfu. Yuǎn yuǎn kàn qù, tāmen gòuchéng le yī fú guānyú shēnghuó de chōuxiàng huà. Báisè de chuángdān xiàng jùdà de huàbù, zài fēng zhōng wēiwēi gǔ qǐ, tóu xià fāngxíng de yīnyǐng. Cǎisè de yīfu xiàng yī chuàn chuàn qízhì, chì chéng huáng lǜ lán zǐ, zài huīsè de lóutǐ bèijǐng xià géwài xiānmíng. Xiǎo háizi de yīfu zuì hǎo rèn, yòu xiǎo yòu duō, yī pái pái guà zhe, xiàng yīnfú yīyàng páiliè de mìmìmámá. Wǒ néng tōngguò liàngshài de yīfu dàzhì tuīduàn chū měi hù rénjiā de jiātíng jiégòu. Sān lóu zuǒbiān nà hù, zǒng shì liàng zhe hěn duō nánshì chènshān hé yīxiē xiǎo qúnzi, yīnggāi shì yī gè fùqīn dài zhe nǚ'ér zhù. Sì lóu zhōngjiān nà hù, liàng de quán shì shēnsè de hòu yīfu, dàgài zhù zhe yī liǎng gè dújū de lǎorén. Wǔ lóu yòubiān nà hù hěn yǒu yìsi, yīfu de fēnggé jīngcháng biànhuà, yǒu shíhou yùndòng zhuāng, yǒu shíhou xīzhuāng, yǒu shíhou huāshào de liányīqún, xiàng shì yī gè yīchú fēngfù de niánqīng nǚxìng. Zhèxiē yīfu zài yángguāng xià mànmàn biàn gān de guòchéng, yǒu yī zhǒng shuō bù chū de níngjìng gǎn. Shuǐfèn yīdiǎn yīdiǎn zhēngfā, zhīwù cóng shēnsè biàn qiǎn, cóng chénzhòng biàn qīngyíng, zuìzhōng zài wēifēng zhōng qīngqīng bǎidòng, xiàng zài hūxī. Liàng yīfu shì zhěnggè chéngshì lǐ zuì ānjìng de jiāwù láodòng, yě shì zuì jùyǒu zhǎnshì xìng de——nǐ bǎ nǐ de shēnghuó guà zài wàimiàn, ràng suǒyǒu rén kàn dào. Dàn méiyǒu rén juéde zhè shì bàolù yǐnsī. Yěxǔ yīnwèi yīfu zài liàng shéng shàng de yàngzi tài píngfán le, píngfán dào chéngwéi le yī zhǒng měi.
text_en: Looking out from my balcony, the balconies of the facing building are draped with all kinds of laundry. From a distance they form an abstract painting about life. White bed sheets like enormous canvases, slightly billowing in the wind, casting rectangular shadows. Colored garments like strings of flags—red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple—strikingly vivid against the gray building backdrop. Children's clothes are easiest to spot: small and numerous, hung in rows, densely arranged like musical notes. I can roughly infer each household's family structure from their laundry. The unit on the left of the third floor always hangs many men's dress shirts and some small dresses—probably a father living with his daughter. The middle unit on the fourth floor hangs only dark, heavy clothing—likely one or two elderly people living alone. The right side of the fifth floor is interesting: clothing styles change frequently—sometimes sportswear, sometimes suits, sometimes flashy dresses—apparently a young woman with a rich wardrobe. Watching these clothes slowly dry in the sunlight has an ineffable serenity. Moisture evaporates bit by bit, fabric shifts from dark to light, from heavy to airy, finally swaying gently in the breeze as if breathing. Hanging laundry is the city's quietest household chore, yet also the most exhibitory—you hang your life outside for everyone to see. But nobody considers it a privacy violation. Perhaps because clothes on a line look too ordinary—so ordinary they become a kind of beauty.

questions.q_zh: 作者怎样判断对面每户人家的家庭结构？
questions.q_en: How does the author determine the family structure of each opposite household?

options.text: 通过观察他们晾晒的衣服的类型和大小
options.pinyin: tōngguò guānchá tāmen liàngshài de yīfu de lèixíng hé dàxiǎo
options.text_en: By observing the types and sizes of their laundry

options.text: 通过跟邻居聊天
options.pinyin: tōngguò gēn línjū liáotiān
options.text_en: By chatting with neighbors

options.text: 通过看他们阳台的装饰
options.pinyin: tōngguò kàn tāmen yángtái de zhuāngshì
options.text_en: By looking at their balcony decorations

questions.q_zh: 为什么作者觉得晾衣服「平凡到成为了一种美」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author think hanging laundry is 'so ordinary it becomes beautiful'?

options.text: 因为衣服的颜色很好看
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yīfu de yánsè hěn hǎokàn
options.text_en: Because the colors of clothes are pretty

options.text: 因为日常生活中最普通的场景有一种不需要刻意追求的自然之美
options.pinyin: yīnwèi rìcháng shēnghuó zhōng zuì pǔtōng de chǎngjǐng yǒu yī zhǒng bù xūyào kèyì zhuīqiú de zìrán zhī měi
options.text_en: Because the most ordinary scenes of daily life have a natural beauty that needs no pursuit

options.text: 因为晾衣服是一种艺术创作
options.pinyin: yīnwèi liàng yīfu shì yī zhǒng yìshù chuàngzuò
options.text_en: Because hanging laundry is a form of artistic creation

title: The Underground Passage Musicians
text_zh: 城市里的地下通道总是有一些卖唱的人。大多数人路过的时候会加快脚步，像是怕被音乐粘住。但我每次经过都会放慢速度，因为在地下通道里听音乐，有一种在任何音乐厅都得不到的体验——回声。地下通道的混凝土墙壁会把声音反射回来，叠加在一起，制造出一种天然的混响效果。一把普通的吉他在地下通道里听起来比在录音棚里更有厚度，一个普通的嗓音在这里听起来比在KTV里更有穿透力。我常去的那个地下通道有一个固定的歌手，一个大约三十岁的男人，总是穿一件旧牛仔外套，坐在一个翻过来的塑料箱子上。他唱的歌都是九十年代的老歌，音准不算完美，但很真诚。每次唱到副歌的时候，他会微微闭上眼睛，好像在回忆什么。我问过他为什么不去参加选秀节目试试运气。他笑了笑说：「我不是要出名，我就是喜欢唱歌。在这里唱，想唱什么就唱什么，没有评委，没有比赛。路过的人要是觉得好听，站一会儿就好；觉得不好听，走掉也没关系。这种自由，上了舞台就没有了。」我觉得他说的「自由」不只是关于唱歌。它是关于一种不被评判的状态——做你喜欢做的事，不需要证明什么，不需要赢得什么。在这个一切都在被打分的时代，这种自由越来越稀有了。
text_pinyin: Chéngshì lǐ de dìxià tōngdào zǒng shì yǒu yīxiē màichàng de rén. Dà duōshù rén lùguò de shíhou huì jiākuài jiǎobù, xiàng shì pà bèi yīnyuè zhān zhù. Dàn wǒ měi cì jīngguò dōu huì fàngmàn sùdù, yīnwèi zài dìxià tōngdào lǐ tīng yīnyuè, yǒu yī zhǒng zài rènhé yīnyuè tīng dōu dé bú dào de tǐyàn——huíshēng. Dìxià tōngdào de hùnníngtǔ qiángbì huì bǎ shēngyīn fǎnshè huílái, diéjiā zài yīqǐ, zhìzào chū yī zhǒng tiānrán de hùnxiǎng xiàoguǒ. Yī bǎ pǔtōng de jítā zài dìxià tōngdào lǐ tīng qǐlái bǐ zài lùyīnpéng lǐ gèng yǒu hòudù, yī gè pǔtōng de sǎngyīn zài zhèlǐ tīng qǐlái bǐ zài KTV lǐ gèng yǒu chuāntòu lì. Wǒ cháng qù de nàge dìxià tōngdào yǒu yī gè gùdìng de gēshǒu, yī gè dàyuē sānshí suì de nánrén, zǒng shì chuān yī jiàn jiù niúzǎi wàitào, zuò zài yī gè fān guòlái de sùliào xiāngzi shàng. Tā chàng de gē dōu shì jiǔshí niándài de lǎo gē, yīnzhǔn bú suàn wánměi, dàn hěn zhēnchéng. Měi cì chàng dào fùgē de shíhou, tā huì wēiwēi bì shàng yǎnjīng, hǎoxiàng zài huíyì shénme. Wǒ wèn guò tā wèishéme bú qù cānjiā xuǎnxiù jiémù shìshi yùnqi. Tā xiào le xiào shuō: 「Wǒ bú shì yào chūmíng, wǒ jiùshì xǐhuan chànggē. Zài zhèlǐ chàng, xiǎng chàng shénme jiù chàng shénme, méiyǒu píngwěi, méiyǒu bǐsài. Lùguò de rén yàoshi juéde hǎotīng, zhàn yīhuìr jiù hǎo; juéde bù hǎotīng, zǒudiào yě méi guānxi. Zhè zhǒng zìyóu, shàng le wǔtái jiù méiyǒu le.」Wǒ juéde tā shuō de「zìyóu」bù zhǐshì guānyú chànggē. Tā shì guānyú yī zhǒng bú bèi píngjuàn de zhuàngtài——zuò nǐ xǐhuan zuò de shì, bù xūyào zhèngmíng shénme, bù xūyào yíngdé shénme. Zài zhège yīqiè dōu zài bèi dǎfēn de shídài, zhè zhǒng zìyóu yuè lái yuè xīyǒu le.
text_en: Underground passages in cities always have buskers. Most people quicken their pace passing by, as if afraid of being stuck by the music. But I always slow down, because hearing music in an underground passage offers an experience no concert hall can match—echo. The concrete walls reflect sound back, layering it to create a natural reverb effect. An ordinary guitar in an underground passage sounds richer than in a recording studio; an ordinary voice sounds more penetrating here than in a karaoke booth. The underpass I frequent has a regular singer—a man around thirty who always wears an old denim jacket, sitting on an overturned plastic crate. He sings nineties songs, his pitch not perfect but sincere. Each time he reaches the chorus, he slightly closes his eyes, as if recalling something. I once asked why he didn't try his luck on a talent show. He smiled and said: 'I'm not trying to get famous—I just like singing. Here I sing whatever I want, no judges, no competition. If passersby like it, they stand awhile; if not, they walk away—no problem. This freedom—you lose it on stage.' I think the 'freedom' he spoke of isn't just about singing. It's about a state of not being judged—doing what you love, needing to prove nothing, needing to win nothing. In this era where everything is being scored, this kind of freedom is increasingly rare.

questions.q_zh: 歌手为什么不去参加选秀节目？
questions.q_en: Why doesn't the singer try out for talent shows?

options.text_en: He doesn't think he sings well enough

options.text: 他珍惜在地下通道唱歌的自由，不想被评判

options.text_en: He values the freedom of singing in the underpass and doesn't want to be judged

options.text: 他没有时间去参加比赛
options.pinyin: tā méiyǒu shíjiān qù cānjiā bǐsài
options.text_en: He doesn't have time to compete

questions.q_zh: 作者认为在地下通道听音乐有什么特别之处？
questions.q_en: What does the author find special about listening to music in underground passages?

options.text: 混凝土墙壁产生天然的混响效果，让普通的声音更有厚度
options.pinyin: hùnníngtǔ qiángbì chǎnshēng tiānrán de hùnxiǎng xiàoguǒ, ràng pǔtōng de shēngyīn gèng yǒu hòudù
options.text_en: Concrete walls create natural reverb, giving ordinary sounds more depth

options.text: 地下通道比较安静
options.pinyin: dìxià tōngdào bǐjiào ānjìng

options.text: 歌手在地下通道唱得更好

options.text_en: Singers perform better in underground passages

title: The Bridge Between Two Districts
text_zh: 我住的地方有一座桥，连接着城市的两个区。桥的这一边是老城区，房子低矮、巷子狭窄、树木茂密，空气里常常带着各种食物的味道。桥的那一边是新开发区，高楼林立、马路宽阔、到处都是玻璃幕墙，干净到有些冷清。我每天骑车过这座桥上班，往返之间感受到两种完全不同的城市气质。早上从老城区出发，经过桥面的时候能感觉到风在变——老城区是暖的、湿润的、混合着烟火气的风；到了桥中间，风变得空旷、干燥；一过桥到新区，风变得冷而直接，带着空调外机排出的热气和新建筑材料的味道。有时候我会在桥的中间停下来，两只手各扶着栏杆的一侧，面朝着河水。这个位置很特别，你能同时听到两边的声音：左边是老城区小贩的叫卖声、电动车的喇叭、谁家的收音机在放戏曲；右边是新区写字楼的空调嗡嗡声、地铁进站的低沉轰鸣、建筑工地的打桩机。两种声音在河面上交汇，被水波搅碎，变成一种不属于任何一边的混合音。我想，也许每座城市都需要这样一座桥，不是为了交通，而是为了让住在两边的人记得：你的世界不是世界的全部。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de dìfāng yǒu yī zuò qiáo, liánjiē zhe chéngshì de liǎng gè qū. Qiáo de zhè yī biān shì lǎo chéngqū, fángzi dīǎi, xiàngzi xiázhǎi, shùmù màomì, kōngqì lǐ chángcháng dài zhe gè zhǒng shíwù de wèidào. Qiáo de nà yī biān shì xīn kāifāqū, gāolóu línlì, mǎlù kuānkuò, dàochù dōu shì bōli mùqiáng, gānjìng dào yǒuxiē lěngqīng. Wǒ měi tiān qí chē guò zhè zuò qiáo shàngbān, wǎngfǎn zhī jiān gǎnshòu dào liǎng zhǒng wánquán bùtóng de chéngshì qìzhì. Zǎoshang cóng lǎo chéngqū chūfā, jīngguò qiáo miàn de shíhou néng gǎnjué dào fēng zài biàn——lǎo chéngqū shì nuǎn de, shīrùn de, hùnhé zhe yānhuǒ qì de fēng; dào le qiáo zhōngjiān, fēng biàn de kōngkuàng, gānzào; yī guò qiáo dào xīn qū, fēng biàn de lěng ér zhíjiē, dài zhe kōngtiáo wàijī pái chū de rèqì hé xīn jiànzhù cáiliào de wèidào. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì zài qiáo de zhōngjiān tíng xiàlái, liǎng zhī shǒu gè fú zhe lángān de yī cè, miàn cháo zhe hé shuǐ. Zhège wèizhì hěn tèbié, nǐ néng tóngshí tīng dào liǎng biān de shēngyīn: zuǒbiān shì lǎo chéngqū xiǎofàn de jiàomài shēng, diàndòng chē de lǎba, shéi jiā de shōuyīnjī zài fàng xìqǔ; yòubiān shì xīn qū xiězìlóu de kōngtiáo wēngwēng shēng, dìtiě jìn zhàn de dīchén hōngmíng, jiànzhù gōngdì de dǎzhuāngjī. Liǎng zhǒng shēngyīn zài hé miàn shàng jiāohuì, bèi shuǐbō jiǎo suì, biànchéng yī zhǒng bù shǔyú rènhé yī biān de hùnhé yīn. Wǒ xiǎng, yěxǔ měi zuò chéngshì dōu xūyào zhèyàng yī zuò qiáo, bú shì wèile jiāotōng, ér shì wèile ràng zhù zài liǎng biān de rén jìde: nǐ de shìjiè bú shì shìjiè de quánbù.
text_en: Where I live there's a bridge connecting two districts. This side is the old town—low buildings, narrow alleys, dense trees, air often carrying the smell of various foods. The other side is the new development zone—towers everywhere, wide roads, glass curtain walls all around, so clean it feels slightly desolate. Every day I cycle across this bridge to work, feeling two completely different urban temperaments on the round trip. Setting out from the old town in the morning, crossing the bridge deck I can feel the wind changing—the old town wind is warm, moist, mixed with the aroma of cooking; at mid-bridge, the wind becomes open and dry; crossing into the new zone, it turns cold and direct, carrying heat from AC units and the smell of new building materials. Sometimes I stop in the middle of the bridge, hands gripping either side of the railing, facing the river. This position is special—you can hear both sides simultaneously: on the left, street vendors calling, electric scooter horns, someone's radio playing opera; on the right, the hum of office building air conditioning, the low rumble of a subway pulling in, the pile driver at a construction site. The two soundscapes converge over the river surface, broken up by ripples into a hybrid sound belonging to neither side. I think perhaps every city needs a bridge like this—not for transportation, but to remind the people living on either side: your world is not the whole world.

questions.q_zh: 作者在桥中间能感受到什么变化？
questions.q_en: What changes does the author feel in the middle of the bridge?

options.text: 风从温暖湿润变得空旷干燥，两边的声音在河面上交汇

options.text_en: Wind shifts from warm and moist to open and dry; sounds from both sides converge over the river

options.text: 交通噪音消失了
options.pinyin: jiāotōng zàoyīn xiāoshī le
options.text_en: Traffic noise disappears

options.text: 温度明显升高
options.pinyin: wēndù míngxiǎn shēnggāo
options.text_en: Temperature rises noticeably

questions.q_zh: 作者认为这座桥的真正意义是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author believe is the bridge's true significance?

options.text: 方便通勤
options.pinyin: fāngbiàn tōngqín
options.text_en: Convenient commuting

options.text: 让人记得自己的世界不是世界的全部
options.pinyin: ràng rén jìde zìjǐ de shìjiè bú shì shìjiè de quánbù
options.text_en: To remind people that their world is not the whole world

options.pinyin: liánjiē xīn jiù liǎng gè qū de jīngjì fāzhǎn
options.text_en: Connecting economic development of old and new districts

title: The Parking Lot at Dawn
text_zh: 凌晨四点的停车场是一个很特别的地方。白天它是功能性的——车来车去，嘈杂、混乱、充满尾气。但在凌晨四点，它变成了一个安静的金属森林。每辆车都沉睡着，车窗上凝结着一层薄薄的露水，在路灯的昏黄光线下泛着微弱的光。那些车的颜色在这种光线下都变成了差不多的深色调——白车变成了灰白，红车变成了暗红，黑车几乎和夜色融为一体。你走在车与车之间的窄道上，脚步声会在金属车身之间产生一种独特的回响，既不像在建筑物之间那样空旷，也不像在室内那样封闭，而是一种介于两者之间的、带着一点金属质感的回声。偶尔一辆车的防盗系统被什么触发了，灯光闪烁几下，嘀嘀叫了两声，然后又安静下去，好像做了一个短暂的梦。我喜欢在这个时间散步，因为这是城市最坦诚的时刻。白天的停车场属于司机们，是一个竞争车位的战场。但凌晨的停车场属于任何一个愿意在这个时间醒着的人——它用沉默欢迎你，不问你为什么会在这里。
text_pinyin: Língchén sì diǎn de tíngchē chǎng shì yī gè hěn tèbié de dìfāng. Báitiān tā shì gōngnéng xìng de——chē lái chē qù, cáozá, hǔnluàn, chōngmǎn wěiqì. Dàn zài língchén sì diǎn, tā biànchéng le yī gè ānjìng de jīnshǔ sēnlín. Měi liàng chē dōu chénshuì zhe, chēchuāng shàng níngjié zhe yī céng báobáo de lùshuǐ, zài lùdēng de hūnhuáng guāngxiàn xià fàn zhe wēiruò de guāng. Nàxiē chē de yánsè zài zhè zhǒng guāngxiàn xià dōu biànchéng le chàbuduō de shēn sèdiào——bái chē biànchéng le huībái, hóng chē biànchéng le ànhóng, hēi chē jīhū hé yèsè róng wéi yītǐ. Nǐ zǒu zài chē yǔ chē zhī jiān de zhǎi dào shàng, jiǎobù shēng huì zài jīnshǔ chēshēn zhī jiān chǎnshēng yī zhǒng dútè de huíxiǎng, jì bù xiàng zài jiànzhùwù zhī jiān nàyàng kōngkuàng, yě bù xiàng zài shìnèi nàyàng fēngbì, ér shì yī zhǒng jiè yú liǎng zhě zhī jiān de, dài zhe yīdiǎn jīnshǔ zhìgǎn de huíshēng. Ǒu'ěr yī liàng chē de fángdào xìtǒng bèi shénme chùfā le, dēngguāng shǎnshuò jǐ xià, dīdī jiào le liǎng shēng, ránhòu yòu ānjìng xiàqù, hǎoxiàng zuò le yī gè duǎnzàn de mèng. Wǒ xǐhuan zài zhège shíjiān sànbù, yīnwèi zhè shì chéngshì zuì tǎnchéng de shíkè. Báitiān de tíngchē chǎng shǔyú sījīmen, shì yī gè jìngzhēng chēwèi de zhànchǎng. Dàn língchén de tíngchē chǎng shǔyú rènhé yī gè yuànyì zài zhège shíjiān xǐng zhe de rén——tā yòng chénmò huānyíng nǐ, bú wèn nǐ wèishéme huì zài zhèlǐ.
text_en: A parking lot at four in the morning is a very special place. By day it's purely functional—cars coming and going, noisy, chaotic, thick with exhaust. But at four a.m., it transforms into a quiet metal forest. Every car sleeps, windows condensed with a thin layer of dew, faintly glinting under the dim yellow streetlights. In this light, all car colors merge into similar dark tones—white cars turn grayish, red cars darken, black cars nearly dissolve into the night. Walking the narrow paths between cars, your footsteps produce a unique resonance among the metal bodies—not as open as between buildings, not as enclosed as indoors, but something in between, carrying a hint of metallic texture. Occasionally a car's alarm is triggered by something—lights flashing a few times, two beeps, then silence again, as if it had a brief dream. I enjoy walking here at this hour because it's the city's most candid moment. By day the parking lot belongs to drivers, a battlefield for competing over spaces. But the predawn parking lot belongs to anyone willing to be awake at this hour—it welcomes you with silence, asking nothing about why you're here.

title: The Renovation Next Door
text_zh: 隔壁人家在装修，已经持续了两个月。每天早上八点半准时开工，电钻声穿透墙壁，把我的整个上午切得七零八碎。一开始我很恼怒。后来我决定不再对抗噪音，而是去理解它。我开始辨别不同的声音代表什么工序。清脆的叮叮声是在敲瓷砖，沉闷的咚咚声是在砸墙，持续的嗡嗡声是电锯在切木板，尖锐的嘶嘶声是在打磨表面。我还能通过声音判断工程的进度：前两周全是砸墙和拆除的声音——暴力而混乱。接下来是水电改造——断断续续的电钻声和水管的敲击声。然后进入瓦工阶段——节奏变得规律了，像有人在有条不紊地做拼图。最近开始的是木工——锯子、刨子、钉枪，声音变得更细致、更有节奏感。装修的声音从破坏性的走向建设性的，从混乱走向秩序。这跟生活的很多事情一样。要创造新东西，你必须先经历一段混乱和破坏的过程。你必须先砸掉旧墙，才能建新的。最不舒服的阶段往往是最初的拆除，但那恰恰是整个过程中最必要的一步。隔壁的装修终于在上周结束了。现在安静了，但我偶尔会怀念那些声音。它们教会了我一种听法：把噪音当作信息来听，把打扰当作过程来理解。
text_pinyin: Gébì rénjiā zài zhuāngxiū, yǐjīng chíxù le liǎng gè yuè. Měi tiān zǎoshang bā diǎn bàn zhǔnshí kāigōng, diànzuān shēng chuāntòu qiángbì, bǎ wǒ de zhěnggè shàngwǔ qiē de qīlíngbāsuì. Yī kāishǐ wǒ hěn nǎonù. Hòulái wǒ juédìng bú zài duìkàng zàoyīn, ér shì qù lǐjiě tā. Wǒ kāishǐ biànbié bùtóng de shēngyīn dàibiǎo shénme gōngxù. Qīngcuì de dīngdīng shēng shì zài qiāo cízhuān, chénmèn de dōngdōng shēng shì zài zá qiáng, chíxù de wēngwēng shēng shì diànjù zài qiē mùbǎn, jiānruì de sīsī shēng shì zài dǎmó biǎomiàn. Wǒ hái néng tōngguò shēngyīn pànduàn gōngchéng de jìndù: qián liǎng zhōu quán shì zá qiáng hé chāichú de shēngyīn——bàolì ér hǔnluàn. Jiēxiàlái shì shuǐdiàn gǎizào——duànduànxùxù de diànzuān shēng hé shuǐguǎn de qiāojī shēng. Ránhòu jìnrù wǎgōng jiēduàn——jiézòu biàn de guīlǜ le, xiàng yǒu rén zài yǒutiáobùwěn de zuò pīntú. Zuìjìn kāishǐ de shì mùgōng——jùzi, bàozi, dīngqiāng, shēngyīn biàn de gèng xìzhì, gèng yǒu jiézòu gǎn. Zhuāngxiū de shēngyīn cóng pòhuài xìng de zǒu xiàng jiànshè xìng de, cóng hǔnluàn zǒu xiàng zhìxù. Zhè gēn shēnghuó de hěn duō shìqing yīyàng. Yào chuàngzào xīn dōngxi, nǐ bìxū xiān jīnglì yī duàn hǔnluàn hé pòhuài de guòchéng. Nǐ bìxū xiān zá diào jiù qiáng, cái néng jiàn xīn de. Zuì bù shūfu de jiēduàn wǎngwǎng shì zuìchū de chāichú, dàn nà qiàqià shì zhěnggè guòchéng zhōng zuì bìyào de yī bù. Gébì de zhuāngxiū zhōngyú zài shàng zhōu jiéshù le. Xiànzài ānjìng le, dàn wǒ ǒu'ěr huì huáiniàn nàxiē shēngyīn. Tāmen jiāohuì le wǒ yī zhǒng tīng fǎ: bǎ zàoyīn dāng zuò xìnxī lái tīng, bǎ dǎrǎo dāng zuò guòchéng lái lǐjiě.
text_en: The neighbors have been renovating for two months now. Every morning at eight-thirty sharp, work begins—drill sounds penetrating the walls, slicing my entire morning to pieces. At first I was furious. Then I decided to stop fighting the noise and try understanding it instead. I began identifying what different sounds represented. Crisp clinking was tile-chipping; muffled thuds meant wall-demolishing; sustained buzzing was the circular saw cutting boards; sharp hissing was surface-sanding. I could even track the project's progress by sound: the first two weeks were all demolition—violent and chaotic. Next came plumbing and electrical—intermittent drilling and pipe-tapping. Then the masonry phase—rhythm becoming regular, like someone methodically assembling a puzzle. Most recently, carpentry—saws, planes, nail guns—sounds growing finer, more rhythmic. The renovation sounds moved from destructive to constructive, from chaos to order. This mirrors many things in life. To create something new, you must first endure a period of chaos and destruction. You must demolish the old wall before building a new one. The most uncomfortable stage is often the initial demolition, yet that's precisely the most necessary step. The renovation next door finally ended last week. It's quiet now, but I occasionally miss those sounds. They taught me a way of listening: treating noise as information, understanding disturbance as process.

title: The Traffic Light No One Obeys
text_zh: 我家附近有一个红绿灯，设在一个很小的路口，两边都是窄巷子，几乎没有车经过。但红绿灯还是忠实地执行着它的职责：红六十秒，绿六十秒，黄三秒，日夜不停地循环。问题是几乎没有人遵守它。行人看一眼没有车，就直接过去了。偶尔经过的骑车人也会减速看一下，然后闯过去。只有最守规矩的老人和带着孩子的家长会认真等绿灯。我曾经做过一个小实验：在那个路口站了一个小时，数了一下遵守红灯的人。结果是：一小时内经过的47个行人中，只有8个等了绿灯。遵守率不到百分之二十。有意思的是，那8个人当中有5个是在有别人的情况下等的。也就是说，大部分人等红灯不是因为认同规则，而是因为有人在看。这让我想到一个问题：当没有人看着你的时候，你还会遵守规则吗？规则的意义到底在哪里——是为了让社会运转，还是为了让我们成为更好的人？也许答案是：一个人是否在无人注视的路口也会等红灯，说明了他跟自己的关系。他不是在等灯，他是在保持一种自我的完整性。不管有没有人看。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā fùjìn yǒu yī gè hónglǜdēng, shè zài yī gè hěn xiǎo de lùkǒu, liǎng biān dōu shì zhǎi xiàngzi, jīhū méiyǒu chē jīngguò. Dàn hónglǜdēng háishi zhōngshí de zhíxíng zhe tā de zhízé: hóng liùshí miǎo, lǜ liùshí miǎo, huáng sān miǎo, rìyè bùtíng de xúnhuán. Wèntí shì jīhū méiyǒu rén zūnshǒu tā. Xíngrén kàn yī yǎn méiyǒu chē, jiù zhíjiē guòqù le. Ǒu'ěr jīngguò de qí chē rén yě huì jiǎnsù kàn yīxià, ránhòu chuǎng guòqù. Zhǐyǒu zuì shǒu guījǔ de lǎorén hé dài zhe háizi de jiāzhǎng huì rènzhēn děng lǜdēng. Wǒ céngjīng zuò guò yī gè xiǎo shíyàn: zài nàge lùkǒu zhàn le yī gè xiǎoshí, shǔ le yīxià zūnshǒu hóngdēng de rén. Jiéguǒ shì: yī xiǎoshí nèi jīngguò de sìshíqī gè xíngrén zhōng, zhǐyǒu bā gè děng le lǜdēng. Zūnshǒu lǜ bú dào bǎifēn zhī èrshí. Yǒu yìsi de shì, nà bā gè rén dāngzhōng yǒu wǔ gè shì zài yǒu biéren de qíngkuàng xià děng de. Yě jiùshì shuō, dà bùfen rén děng hóngdēng bú shì yīnwèi rèntóng guīzé, ér shì yīnwèi yǒu rén zài kàn. Zhè ràng wǒ xiǎng dào yī gè wèntí: dāng méiyǒu rén kàn zhe nǐ de shíhou, nǐ hái huì zūnshǒu guīzé ma? Guīzé de yìyì dàodǐ zài nǎlǐ——shì wèile ràng shèhuì yùnzhuǎn, háishi wèile ràng wǒmen chéngwéi gèng hǎo de rén? Yěxǔ dá'àn shì: yī gè rén shìfǒu zài wú rén zhùshì de lùkǒu yě huì děng hóngdēng, shuōmíng le tā gēn zìjǐ de guānxi. Tā bú shì zài děng dēng, tā shì zài bǎochí yī zhǒng zìwǒ de wánzhěng xìng. Bùguǎn yǒu méiyǒu rén kàn.
text_en: Near my home there's a traffic light at a very small intersection, with narrow alleys on both sides and almost no cars. Yet the light faithfully performs its duty: red sixty seconds, green sixty seconds, yellow three seconds, cycling day and night. The problem is almost nobody obeys it. Pedestrians glance for cars, then cross. The occasional cyclist slows slightly and runs the light. Only the most rule-abiding elderly and parents with children actually wait for green. I once conducted a small experiment: I stood at that intersection for an hour and counted red-light compliance. Result: of forty-seven pedestrians who passed, only eight waited for green. Compliance rate under twenty percent. Interestingly, five of those eight waited only when others were around. Meaning most people wait at red lights not because they accept the rule, but because someone is watching. This raises a question: when nobody is watching, do you still follow the rules? Where does the meaning of rules actually lie—to make society function, or to make us better people? Perhaps the answer is: whether a person waits at a red light when nobody is watching reveals their relationship with themselves. They're not waiting for the light—they're maintaining a kind of personal integrity. Regardless of whether anyone is looking.

questions.q_en: What interesting finding did the author's experiment reveal?

title: The Old Tree in the Courtyard
text_zh: 我们小区中央有一棵巨大的香樟树，据说已经有六七十年的历史了。它的树干需要两个成年人才能合抱，树冠铺开来像一把撑开的巨伞，遮住了将近半个院子的天空。这棵树是整个小区的公共客厅。春天的时候，树下围着一圈长椅，老人们坐在那里晒太阳聊天，小孩子在树根间的缝隙里找虫子。夏天，它提供全小区最好的阴凉，比任何空调都舒服，因为风吹过树叶的时候会自动降温，带来一种空调给不了的清新。秋天，树叶开始掉落，地上铺了一层金色的地毯，物业每天扫两遍，但永远扫不完。冬天，树叶掉光了，只剩下粗壮的枝干，像一幅水墨画。前几年有一次，开发商想在院子里加盖一个车棚，需要把这棵树移走。消息一出来，整个小区的居民都反对了。不是因为环保意识，不是因为风水考虑，而是因为每个人的记忆里都有这棵树。在树下乘凉的夏天，在树下打牌的午后，在树下被求婚的傍晚，在树下教孩子骑自行车的周末。一棵树就是一个社区的共同记忆存储器。砍掉它，就像格式化了整个小区的硬盘。最后那个车棚没有建。开发商让步了，不是因为他们理解了树的意义，而是因为他们无法承受全体居民的反对。但结果是一样的。树还在。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū zhōngyāng yǒu yī kē jùdà de xiāngzhāng shù, jùshuō yǐjīng yǒu liù qī shí nián de lìshǐ le. Tā de shùgàn xūyào liǎng gè chéngnián rén cái néng hé bào, shùguān pū kāi lái xiàng yī bǎ chēng kāi de jù sǎn, zhē zhù le jiāngjìn bàn gè yuànzi de tiānkōng. Zhè kē shù shì zhěnggè xiǎoqū de gōnggòng kètīng. Chūntiān de shíhou, shù xià wéi zhe yī quān chángyǐ, lǎorénmen zuò zài nàlǐ shài tàiyáng liáotiān, xiǎo háizi zài shùgēn jiān de fèngxì lǐ zhǎo chóngzi. Xiàtiān, tā tígōng quán xiǎoqū zuì hǎo de yīnliáng, bǐ rènhé kōngtiáo dōu shūfu, yīnwèi fēng chuī guò shùyè de shíhou huì zìdòng jiàngwēn, dài lái yī zhǒng kōngtiáo gěi bù liǎo de qīngxīn. Qiūtiān, shùyè kāishǐ diàoluò, dì shàng pū le yī céng jīnsè de dìtǎn, wùyè měi tiān sǎo liǎng biàn, dàn yǒngyuǎn sǎo bù wán. Dōngtiān, shùyè diào guāng le, zhǐ shèng xià cūzhuàng de zhīgàn, xiàng yī fú shuǐmò huà. Qián jǐ nián yǒu yī cì, kāifāshāng xiǎng zài yuànzi lǐ jiā gài yī gè chēpéng, xūyào bǎ zhè kē shù yízǒu. Xiāoxi yī chūlái, zhěnggè xiǎoqū de jūmín dōu fǎnduì le. Bú shì yīnwèi huánbǎo yìshí, bú shì yīnwèi fēngshuǐ kǎolǜ, ér shì yīnwèi měi gè rén de jìyì lǐ dōu yǒu zhè kē shù. Zài shù xià chéngliáng de xiàtiān, zài shù xià dǎpái de wǔhòu, zài shù xià bèi qiúhūn de bàngwǎn, zài shù xià jiāo háizi qí zìxíngchē de zhōumò. Yī kē shù jiùshì yī gè shèqū de gòngtóng jìyì cúnchǔ qì. Kǎn diào tā, jiù xiàng géshìhuà le zhěnggè xiǎoqū de yìngpán. Zuìhòu nàge chēpéng méiyǒu jiàn. Kāifāshāng ràngbù le, bú shì yīnwèi tāmen lǐjiě le shù de yìyì, ér shì yīnwèi tāmen wúfǎ chéngshòu quántǐ jūmín de fǎnduì. Dàn jiéguǒ shì yīyàng de. Shù hái zài.
text_en: In the center of our residential compound stands a massive camphor tree, said to be sixty or seventy years old. Its trunk requires two adults to embrace, its canopy spreading like an opened giant umbrella, shading nearly half the courtyard's sky. This tree is the entire compound's public living room. In spring, a circle of benches surrounds it; elders sit there sunbathing and chatting while children search for insects in the gaps between roots. In summer, it provides the compound's best shade—more comfortable than any air conditioning, because wind through its leaves cools naturally, bringing a freshness AC cannot. In autumn, leaves begin falling, carpeting the ground in gold; the property management sweeps twice daily but can never finish. In winter, bare of leaves, only stout branches remain, like an ink-wash painting. A few years ago, a developer wanted to build a car shelter in the courtyard, requiring the tree's removal. The moment word got out, every resident opposed it. Not from environmental awareness, not from feng shui concerns, but because this tree existed in everyone's memories. Summers cooling under it, afternoons playing cards beneath it, evenings of proposals under it, weekends teaching children to ride bicycles under it. A tree is a community's shared memory storage device. Cutting it down would be like formatting the entire compound's hard drive. In the end, the car shelter wasn't built. The developer conceded—not because they understood the tree's meaning, but because they couldn't withstand unanimous opposition. But the result was the same. The tree remains.

questions.q_zh: 居民们为什么反对移走那棵树？
questions.q_en: Why did residents oppose removing the tree?

options.text: 因为它有环保价值
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā yǒu huánbǎo jiàzhí
options.text_en: Because of its environmental value

options.text: 因为每个人的记忆里都有这棵树，它是社区的共同记忆
options.pinyin: yīnwèi měi gè rén de jìyì lǐ dōu yǒu zhè kē shù, tā shì shèqū de gòngtóng jìyì
options.text_en: Because this tree exists in everyone's memories; it's the community's shared memory

options.text: 因为树的风水对小区很重要
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shù de fēngshuǐ duì xiǎoqū hěn zhòngyào
options.text_en: Because the tree's feng shui is important to the compound

questions.q_en: What does the author compare cutting down the tree to?

options.text: 格式化整个小区的硬盘
options.pinyin: géshìhuà zhěnggè xiǎoqū de yìngpán
options.text_en: Formatting the entire compound's hard drive

options.text: 拆掉一座房子
options.pinyin: chāi diào yī zuò fángzi
options.text_en: Demolishing a house

options.text: 撕掉一本相册
options.pinyin: sī diào yī běn xiàngcè
options.text_en: Tearing up a photo album

title: The View from a Rooftop
text_zh: 我有时候会爬上我们楼顶的天台去坐一会儿。那里没有什么风景可看——四面都是差不多高的居民楼，远处是灰蒙蒙的天际线——但我喜欢的不是看风景，而是换一个角度看日常。从天台往下看，你会发现街道上的人变得很小，小到你看不清他们的表情，分辨不出他们是开心还是焦虑。汽车变成了缓慢移动的彩色方块，公交车像一条条笨重的毛毛虫在马路上蠕动。这个角度让一切都变得不那么紧迫了。你不会再去想那个还没回复的邮件、那笔还没还清的贷款、那段不知道怎么处理的关系。因为从这个高度看，那些东西实在太小了。不是不重要，是它们重新回到了它们本来的比例。我们平时之所以觉得问题很大，是因为我们离它太近了。就像你把一个拇指放在眼前，它能挡住整个太阳。但退后一步，你就会发现太阳其实比你的拇指大一百万倍。天台给我的不是逃避，而是距离。有时候解决问题的方法不是更努力地思考，而是退远一点看。你在地面上绕不出去的迷宫，换到天台上一看就清楚了：出口一直在那里，只是你站得太低看不到。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu shíhou huì pá shàng wǒmen lóu dǐng de tiāntái qù zuò yīhuìr. Nàlǐ méiyǒu shénme fēngjǐng kě kàn——sìmiàn dōu shì chàbuduō gāo de jūmínlóu, yuǎnchù shì huīméngméng de tiānjìxiàn——dàn wǒ xǐhuan de bú shì kàn fēngjǐng, ér shì huàn yī gè jiǎodù kàn rìcháng. Cóng tiāntái wǎng xià kàn, nǐ huì fāxiàn jiēdào shàng de rén biàn de hěn xiǎo, xiǎo dào nǐ kàn bù qīng tāmen de biǎoqíng, fēnbiàn bù chū tāmen shì kāixīn háishi jiāolǜ. Qìchē biànchéng le huǎnmàn yídòng de cǎisè fāngkuài, gōngjiāo chē xiàng yī tiáo tiáo bènzhòng de máomao chóng zài mǎlù shàng rúdòng. Zhège jiǎodù ràng yīqiè dōu biàn de bú nàme jǐnpò le. Nǐ bú huì zài qù xiǎng nàge hái méi huífù de yóujiàn, nà bǐ hái méi huánqīng de dàikuǎn, nà duàn bù zhīdào zěnme chǔlǐ de guānxi. Yīnwèi cóng zhège gāodù kàn, nàxiē dōngxi shízài tài xiǎo le. Bú shì bú zhòngyào, shì tāmen chóngxīn huí dào le tāmen běnlái de bǐlì. Wǒmen píngshí zhī suǒyǐ juéde wèntí hěn dà, shì yīnwèi wǒmen lí tā tài jìn le. Jiù xiàng nǐ bǎ yī gè mǔzhǐ fàng zài yǎn qián, tā néng dǎng zhù zhěnggè tàiyáng. Dàn tuìhòu yī bù, nǐ jiù huì fāxiàn tàiyáng qíshí bǐ nǐ de mǔzhǐ dà yī bǎi wàn bèi. Tiāntái gěi wǒ de bú shì táobì, ér shì jùlí. Yǒu shíhou jiějué wèntí de fāngfǎ bú shì gèng nǔlì de sīkǎo, ér shì tuì yuǎn yīdiǎn kàn. Nǐ zài dìmiàn shàng rào bù chūqù de mígōng, huàn dào tiāntái shàng yī kàn jiù qīngchu le: chūkǒu yīzhí zài nàlǐ, zhǐshì nǐ zhàn de tài dī kàn bú dào.
text_en: Sometimes I climb to the rooftop terrace of our building and sit for a while. There's nothing scenic to see—similar-height residential buildings on all sides, a hazy skyline in the distance—but what I enjoy isn't the view but seeing daily life from a different angle. Looking down from the rooftop, you notice the people on the street have become very small—too small to read their expressions, to distinguish whether they're happy or anxious. Cars become slowly moving colored blocks; buses crawl along the road like clumsy caterpillars. This angle makes everything feel less urgent. You stop thinking about that unanswered email, that unpaid loan, that relationship you don't know how to handle. Because from this height, those things are really very small. Not unimportant—they've simply returned to their actual proportions. The reason we normally think problems are huge is that we're too close to them. Like holding a thumb in front of your eye—it can block the entire sun. But step back and you realize the sun is actually a million times larger than your thumb. What the rooftop gives me isn't escape but distance. Sometimes the way to solve a problem isn't thinking harder but stepping further back. The maze you can't navigate at ground level becomes clear from the rooftop: the exit has been there all along—you were just standing too low to see it.

questions.q_zh: 作者上天台的目的是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the author's purpose in going to the rooftop?

options.text: 欣赏城市的美景
options.pinyin: xīnshǎng chéngshì de měijǐng
options.text_en: To appreciate the city's beautiful scenery

options.text: 换一个角度看日常，获得距离感，让问题回到本来的比例
options.pinyin: huàn yī gè jiǎodù kàn rìcháng, huòdé jùlí gǎn, ràng wèntí huí dào běnlái de bǐlì
options.text_en: To see daily life from a different angle, gain distance, and let problems return to their true proportions

options.text: 锻炼身体
options.pinyin: duànliàn shēntǐ
options.text_en: To exercise

questions.q_zh: 拇指挡住太阳的比喻说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the thumb-blocking-the-sun metaphor illustrate?

options.text: 我们的视力有局限性
options.pinyin: wǒmen de shìlì yǒu júxiàn xìng
options.text_en: Our vision has limitations

options.text: 当我们离问题太近时，问题会显得比实际大得多
options.pinyin: dāng wǒmen lí wèntí tài jìn shí, wèntí huì xiǎn de bǐ shíjì dà de duō
options.text_en: When we're too close to a problem, it appears much larger than it actually is

options.text: 太阳比拇指大很多
options.pinyin: tàiyáng bǐ mǔzhǐ dà hěn duō
options.text_en: The sun is much bigger than a thumb
